text: When designing a REST API, following best practices makes your system reliable and easy to use. First, create clear, logical endpoints that represent resources, like `/users` or `/products`. Use standard HTTP status codes—for example, `200 OK` for successful requests, `404 Not Found` when a resource doesn’t exist, and `500 Internal Server Error` for server problems. This helps developers understand what happened. Good documentation is essential; explain each endpoint, its expected inputs, and the responses it can return. Finally, always include versioning in your API, such as `/v1/users`. This allows you to update the API over time without breaking existing applications that depend on older versions.
